panel.origin: The first First Lady of the United States (1789–1797), wife of George Washington, who defined a role with no precedent or title.
panel.mixology: Martha set the template for the position, hosting formal receptions that balanced republican simplicity with the dignity of the new nation.
panel.examples: She was not yet called 'First Lady'; that term emerged later. Contemporaries addressed her as 'Lady Washington,' inventing the role as she went.

panel.origin: The second First Lady (1797–1801), wife of John Adams, and one of the most politically influential women of the founding generation.
panel.mixology: Abigail's extensive letters to her husband, urging him to 'remember the ladies,' make her an early voice for women's rights.
panel.examples: She was the first First Lady to live in the White House, hanging laundry in the unfinished East Room of the brand-new, drafty mansion.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1809–1817), wife of James Madison, remembered as one of the most beloved and consequential hostesses in the role's history.
panel.mixology: Dolley famously saved a portrait of George Washington from the White House before the British burned it in 1814.
panel.examples: Her vibrant social gatherings helped define Washington political culture, and she effectively served as hostess for the widower Jefferson as well.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1825–1829), wife of John Quincy Adams, and the only First Lady born outside the United States until the 21st century.
panel.mixology: Born in London, Louisa was the only foreign-born First Lady for nearly two centuries, until Melania Trump.
panel.examples: Her detailed memoirs offer a rare, candid window into the loneliness and political pressures of life in the early White House.

panel.origin: The wife of Andrew Jackson, who died weeks after his 1828 election victory and never served as First Lady.
panel.mixology: Rachel died before the inauguration, and her niece Emily Donelson served as White House hostess in her place.
panel.examples: Vicious attacks on her marriage during the bitter campaign were blamed by Jackson for her death, fueling his lifelong fury at his political enemies.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1845–1849), wife of James K. Polk, an unusually engaged political partner and adviser to her husband.
panel.mixology: Sarah served as Polk's private secretary and closest political confidante, a substantive role rare for the era.
panel.examples: A devout Presbyterian, she banned dancing and hard liquor at White House functions, lending the Polk administration a notably austere tone.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1861–1865), wife of Abraham Lincoln, whose tenure was marked by the Civil War and personal tragedy.
panel.mixology: Mary endured the deaths of her sons and witnessed her husband's assassination at her side, suffering profound grief throughout.
panel.examples: Often unfairly maligned in her time, modern historians have reassessed her as a complex, intelligent woman crushed by extraordinary loss.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1869–1877), wife of Ulysses S. Grant, who presided over a glittering post-war White House social scene.
panel.mixology: Julia later called her White House years 'the happiest period' of her life, relishing the role's social prominence.
panel.examples: Her memoirs were the first ever written by a First Lady, though they remained unpublished until well over a century later.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1877–1881), wife of Rutherford B. Hayes, the first to hold a college degree.
panel.mixology: Lucy was the first First Lady to be a college graduate, signaling a shift in expectations for women in public life.
panel.examples: Her ban on alcohol in the White House earned her the nickname 'Lemonade Lucy,' though the moniker was coined only after her time.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1886–1889, 1893–1897), wife of Grover Cleveland, and the youngest First Lady in history.
panel.mixology: Frances was just 21 when she married the president in the White House, the only First Lady wed in the executive mansion.
panel.examples: Her immense popularity made her an early celebrity, with her image used, without permission, to sell countless commercial products.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1897–1901), wife of William McKinley, who served despite chronic illness and frequent seizures.
panel.mixology: Ida defied medical convention by remaining at her husband's side at public events despite her epilepsy.
panel.examples: The president's tender, attentive care for his ailing wife was widely noted and admired throughout his time in office.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1901–1909), second wife of Theodore Roosevelt, who modernized and professionalized the role.
panel.mixology: Edith was the first First Lady to employ a full-time social secretary, formalizing the office's growing duties.
panel.examples: She skillfully managed both a boisterous White House full of children and her famously energetic husband, bringing order to the chaos.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1909–1913), wife of William Howard Taft, who left an enduring mark on the capital's landscape.
panel.mixology: Helen was instrumental in bringing Japan's gift of cherry trees to Washington, D.C., now a beloved springtime spectacle.
panel.examples: An ambitious political partner, she suffered a stroke early in her tenure but recovered to resume an active public role.

panel.origin: The first wife of Woodrow Wilson and First Lady (1913–1914), an accomplished painter who died in office.
panel.mixology: Ellen used her position to advocate for slum-clearance housing reform in Washington before her death in 1914.
panel.examples: A serious landscape artist, she was among the most genuinely talented painters ever to serve as First Lady.

panel.origin: The second wife of Woodrow Wilson and First Lady (1915–1921), who took on extraordinary influence after his stroke.
panel.mixology: After Wilson's 1919 stroke, Edith controlled access to him, screening matters of state in what critics called a 'stewardship.'
panel.examples: Some historians describe her as effectively the nation's first acting female leader, though she insisted she merely managed her husband's affairs.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1921–1923), wife of Warren Harding, an assertive partner who shaped her husband's career and image.
panel.mixology: Florence, who called her husband 'Wurr'n,' was a shrewd political operator who helped manage his rise to the presidency.
panel.examples: A pioneering figure, she embraced the press and new media, hosting garden parties for veterans and projecting a modern public persona.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1923–1929), wife of Calvin Coolidge, whose warmth provided a vivid contrast to her famously reserved husband.
panel.mixology: Grace's vivacious charm balanced 'Silent Cal's' austerity, making her one of the most popular First Ladies of her time.
panel.examples: A former teacher of deaf students, she brought a lively spirit and a beloved pet raccoon named Rebecca to the White House.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1929–1933), wife of Herbert Hoover, a geologist and linguist of remarkable accomplishment.
panel.mixology: Lou was fluent in several languages and, with her husband, translated a 16th-century Latin mining text into English.
panel.examples: She was the first First Lady to deliver regular national radio addresses, pioneering the role's use of broadcast media.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1933–1945), wife of Franklin D. Roosevelt, widely considered the most influential First Lady in history.
panel.mixology: Eleanor transformed the role into a platform for activism, holding her own press conferences and championing civil and human rights.
panel.examples: After the White House, she chaired the UN commission that drafted the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, earning the title 'First Lady of the World.'

panel.origin: The First Lady (1945–1953), wife of Harry Truman, who valued privacy and shunned the public spotlight.
panel.mixology: Bess was a trusted private adviser to her husband, who called her 'the Boss,' though she avoided public political roles.
panel.examples: A sharp contrast to her activist predecessor Eleanor Roosevelt, she deliberately kept a low profile throughout her tenure.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1953–1961), wife of Dwight Eisenhower, a popular hostess who embodied 1950s domestic ideals.
panel.mixology: Mamie's warm, traditional style and signature pink wardrobe made her a beloved and fashionable national figure.
panel.examples: Having moved dozens of times as a military wife, she relished finally settling into a permanent home in the White House.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1961–1963), wife of John F. Kennedy, who brought unprecedented glamour, culture, and style to the role.
panel.mixology: Jacqueline led a historic restoration of the White House and gave a famous televised tour of its restored rooms.
panel.examples: Her grace and composure after her husband's assassination, and her crafting of his 'Camelot' legacy, made her an enduring global icon.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1963–1969), wife of Lyndon B. Johnson, a savvy businesswoman and pioneering environmental advocate.
panel.mixology: Lady Bird championed a national beautification and conservation campaign, a forerunner of the modern environmental movement.
panel.examples: She was the first First Lady to have her own press secretary and chief of staff, and a successful businesswoman in her own right.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1969–1974), wife of Richard Nixon, who promoted volunteerism and undertook significant solo diplomacy.
panel.mixology: Pat traveled extensively as a goodwill ambassador, becoming the most-traveled First Lady up to that point.
panel.examples: She championed personal volunteer service as 'the spirit of America,' though her tenure ended under the shadow of Watergate.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1974–1977), wife of Gerald Ford, celebrated for her candor on issues long considered taboo.
panel.mixology: Betty spoke openly about her breast cancer and mastectomy, dramatically raising public awareness of the disease.
panel.examples: Her later openness about her own addiction led to the founding of the Betty Ford Center, a landmark in addiction treatment.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1977–1981), wife of Jimmy Carter, a close governing partner and tireless mental health advocate.
panel.mixology: Rosalynn sat in on Cabinet meetings and championed mental health reform, an unusually substantive policy role.
panel.examples: Her decades of humanitarian work alongside her husband after leaving office redefined the post-presidency for both of them.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1981–1989), wife of Ronald Reagan, known for her protective devotion to her husband and her anti-drug campaign.
panel.mixology: Nancy's 'Just Say No' campaign became one of the most recognized anti-drug efforts in American history.
panel.examples: A fiercely loyal guardian of her husband's interests, she wielded quiet but significant influence over his staff and schedule.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1989–1993), wife of George H. W. Bush, a beloved figure who championed family literacy.
panel.mixology: Barbara made universal literacy her signature cause, founding a foundation still devoted to the effort.
panel.examples: She is one of only two women in American history to be both the wife and the mother of a president, a rare distinction.

panel.origin: The First Lady (1993–2001), wife of Bill Clinton, who later forged a singular political career of her own.
panel.mixology: Hillary took an active policy role, leading a major health-care reform effort early in her husband's first term.
panel.examples: She is the only First Lady to later hold high office herself, becoming a senator, secretary of state, and a major-party presidential nominee.

panel.origin: The First Lady (2001–2009), wife of George W. Bush, a former teacher and librarian who promoted education and literacy.
panel.mixology: Laura, a trained librarian, made education, reading, and literacy the central focus of her time in the role.
panel.examples: Her calm, steady public presence was widely noted in the difficult period following the September 11 attacks.

panel.origin: The First Lady (2009–2017), wife of Barack Obama, the first Black First Lady, who became a global cultural figure.
panel.mixology: Michelle championed health and nutrition through her 'Let's Move!' campaign and support for military families and education.
panel.examples: A Princeton and Harvard Law graduate, her bestselling memoir 'Becoming' set sales records and cemented her broad public influence.

FOUNDING ERA.en: The term 'First Lady' didn't exist at first — Martha Washington was simply called 'Lady Washington' as she invented the role.

19TH CENTURY.en: Frances Cleveland was just 21 when she married in the White House, making her the youngest First Lady in history.

PROGRESSIVE ERA.en: After Woodrow Wilson's stroke, Edith Wilson controlled access to him so tightly that some called her the nation's first acting female leader.

MID-CENTURY.en: Eleanor Roosevelt held her own press conferences and later helped draft the UN's Universal Declaration of Human Rights.

MODERN ERA.en: Betty Ford's openness about addiction led to the founding of the Betty Ford Center, transforming how America treats addiction.
